title: Calamares a la Romana
subtitle: Spanish battered squid rings
excerpt: Squid rings dipped in a light, fizzing batter of flour and sparkling water and fried until crisp, pale gold, and still tender inside. Eaten immediately with lemon and sea salt.
sourceNotes: Calamares a la romana (Roman-style squid) are a classic Spanish tapa, found across the country but particularly associated with Madrid's tasco bars. Documented in Penelope Casas, Tapas: The Little Dishes of Spain (1985).

content.text: The batter is what makes calamares a la romana distinct. It is thin, barely-there, and slightly crisp, more a coating than a shell. The carbonation in sparkling water is responsible: the bubbles create a lightness that flour and water alone cannot achieve. The squid inside must stay tender, which means hot oil and quick cooking: no more than 2 minutes per batch.

Dry the squid rings as thoroughly as you can before battering. Surface moisture causes the batter to steam rather than crisp and makes it slip off. Pat them with kitchen paper and leave them on a rack for a few minutes before coating.

items.name: Squid
items.prepNote: cleaned and cut into 1 cm rings; tentacles halved

content.text: If starting from whole squid, pull the head away from the body, remove the plastic-like quill, and peel the purple membrane from the outside. Cut the body into 1 cm rings; halve the tentacles. Pat everything completely dry with kitchen paper. Leave on a rack or board for 15 to 30 minutes.

Whisk the flour, salt, and paprika (if using) together. Add the cold sparkling water and whisk until just combined, a few lumps are fine, do not over-mix. Heat the oil in a deep saucepan to 180°C. Working in batches of 6 to 8 rings, dip the squid into the batter, let the excess drip off, and lower into the oil. Fry for 1.5 to 2 minutes until pale gold. Do not crowd the pan. Drain on kitchen paper and immediately season with sea salt flakes. Serve at once with lemon.

items.fix: Pat dry obsessively before battering; let the rings sit on a rack for 20 minutes after patting
items.cause: Squid not dry enough; excess moisture prevents adhesion

items.fix: Fry for no more than 2 minutes; the rings should be cooked through but still tender
items.cause: Cooked too long; squid toughens quickly with prolonged heat

items.fix: The batter should be the consistency of single cream; thin with a splash more sparkling water if needed
items.cause: Too much flour relative to water, or over-mixed
items.symptom: Batter is too thick and doughy

content.text: In Madrid, a bocadillo de calamares (a crusty roll filled with fried squid rings) is street food as ubiquitous as a hot dog. The same rings, served on a plate with lemon, are the standard tapa. The Roman-style name refers not to Rome but to the batter style, which arrived in Spain via Catholic religious observance, fried fish on meatless days.
